messages.content: The CSS and SASS files import from the version Next. js 9.1

In Next.js 9.1, CSS and SASS files can be imported into pages and components using the `import` statement. Here's an example of importing a CSS file: ```javascript import styles from './styles.module.css' export default function MyComponent() { return
Hello world
} ``` And here's an example of importing a SASS file: ```javascript import styles from './styles.module.scss' export default function MyComponent() { return
Hello world
} ``` With this approach, the styles are scoped to the component and won't affect other elements on the page. In addition to importing individual files, Next.js also supports importing global CSS and SASS files using the `pages/_app.js` file. This file is run on every page, so any global styles imported here will be applied to every page. ```javascript import '../styles/global.css' export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) { return } ``` It's also possible to configure Next.js to use other CSS preprocessors, like Less or Stylus, by installing the corresponding package and configuring webpack. However, the basic approach for importing styles remains the same.

The `getInitialProps` method in Next.js is a special method that allows a Next.js page component to fetch and load data before rendering on the client-side. This method is commonly used to fetch data from an external API or database, as well as to render a page on the server side.

In the given example, the `getInitialProps` method is defined as a static method on the `ProductsPage` component. The purpose of this method is to fetch any necessary data for the `ProductsPage` component before it is rendered on the client-side.

The method returns an object with data that will be assigned to the component's props. In this case, the method returns an empty object `{}`. If the method returned a value such as `{products: []}`, the ProductsPage component would receive a prop named `products` with an empty array as its initial value.

Next.js automatically calls the `getInitialProps` method when the page loads and passes the current context as a parameter. The context object contains information about the current request, including the request and response objects, as well as the URL and query parameters.

The `withRouter` higher-order component is used to include router properties and methods as props to the component. This allows the component to access the current route and query parameters using `this.props.router`. 

In summary, the `getInitialProps` method is a powerful feature in Next.js that allows for server-side rendering and the pre-fetching of necessary data for a page before it is rendered on the client-side.
How can we customize title on tab bar and change meta tag for per page in Next js?

In Next.js, you can use the `head` component from `next/head` to customize the title and meta tags for individual pages. To customize the title on the tab bar, you can use the `title` tag within the `head` component. Here's an example: ```javascript import Head from 'next/head' export default function MyPage() { return (
Custom Page Title {/* Page content goes here */}
) } ``` This will set the title on the tab bar to "Custom Page Title" for the `MyPage` component. To change the meta tags, you can use other tags within the `head` component. For instance, you can use the `meta` tag to set the description and keywords for the page: ```javascript Custom Page Title ``` Additionally, you can also set other meta tags like Open Graph meta tags for social media sharing. Here's an example: ```javascript Custom Page Title ``` This will customize the title, description, and image that are shown when someone shares the page on social media. By using the `head` component in Next.js, you can easily customize the title and meta tags for individual pages.
You set a new component in the /home/ folder instead of being in the ..components/ folder. Can that component be used?

Yes, components located in any folder within the `pages` directory in Next.js can be used as a page or a subpage. When a file is added to the `pages` directory in Next.js, it becomes a route that can be used to serve the page. For example, if you add a file named `home.js` within the `pages` directory, the page will be accessible at the `/home` route. In the given scenario, if you create a new component within the `/home` folder (e.g. `/pages/home/NewComponent.js`), it can be used and imported into any page or component within the `pages` directory (including the pages nested within other directories). For example, you could import `NewComponent` within `/pages/index.js` like so: ```javascript import NewComponent from './home/NewComponent' export default function HomePage() { return (

Welcome to my site

) } ``` You can use any file in the `pages` directory as a page or a subpage in Next.js, regardless of its location within the directory.

IN JavaScript 9, Why is checking for null a good practice?

In JavaScript, checking for null is considered a good practice because it helps prevent certain types of errors.

For example, if a variable is expected to contain an object but is instead null, attempting to access a property or method on the variable will result in a "TypeError" and may cause the application to crash. By checking if the variable is null before attempting to access its properties or methods, you can prevent these errors from happening and handle the situation appropriately.

Similarly, when passing arguments to a function, it's important to check if the argument is null before attempting to use it. This is especially important if the function expects an object or array argument, as attempting to access properties or elements of a null value will result in errors. By checking for null first, you can prevent these types of errors from happening and handle the situation appropriately.

Overall, checking for null is good practice in JavaScript because it helps prevent errors and ensures that values are properly validated before they are used.
